category.text: $course$/SDS4PH/Module 2 - Exploratory Health Data Analysis with R

name.text: M2Q01: In the tidyverse, the pipe operator |> is used to:

questiontext.text: In the tidyverse, the pipe operator |> is used to:

generalfeedback.text: The pipe operator (|> or %>%) chains operations by passing the output of one function as the first argument of the next, making code read left-to-right like a sentence. Example: df |> filter(x > 5) |> summarize(mean(x))

answer.text: Pass the result of the left-hand expression as the first argument to the right-hand function

feedback.text: Correct! The pipe operator (|> or %>%) chains operations by passing the output of one function as the first argument of the next, making code read left-to-right like a sentence. Example: df |> filter(x > 5) |> summarize(mean(x))

Incorrect. The pipe operator (|> or %>%) chains operations by passing the output of one function as the first argument of the next, making code read left-to-right like a sentence. Example: df |> filter(x > 5) |> summarize(mean(x))

Incorrect. The pipe operator (|> or %>%) chains operations by passing the output of one function as the first argument of the next, making code read left-to-right like a sentence. Example: df |> filter(x > 5) |> summarize(mean(x))

Incorrect. The pipe operator (|> or %>%) chains operations by passing the output of one function as the first argument of the next, making code read left-to-right like a sentence. Example: df |> filter(x > 5) |> summarize(mean(x))

name.text: M2Q02: Which dplyr function would you use to create a new column called ‘cfr’ (case fat

questiontext.text: Which dplyr function would you use to create a new column called ‘cfr’ (case fatality ratio) from existing columns ‘deaths’ and ‘cases’?

generalfeedback.text: mutate() adds new columns or modifies existing ones while preserving all other columns. filter() subsets rows, summarize() collapses to summary statistics, and select() chooses columns.

feedback.text: Correct! mutate() adds new columns or modifies existing ones while preserving all other columns. filter() subsets rows, summarize() collapses to summary statistics, and select() chooses columns.
Incorrect. mutate() adds new columns or modifies existing ones while preserving all other columns. filter() subsets rows, summarize() collapses to summary statistics, and select() chooses columns.
Incorrect. mutate() adds new columns or modifies existing ones while preserving all other columns. filter() subsets rows, summarize() collapses to summary statistics, and select() chooses columns.
Incorrect. mutate() adds new columns or modifies existing ones while preserving all other columns. filter() subsets rows, summarize() collapses to summary statistics, and select() chooses columns.

name.text: M2Q03: What is the difference between summarize() and mutate() in dplyr?

questiontext.text: What is the difference between summarize() and mutate() in dplyr?

generalfeedback.text: summarize() reduces the data to summary statistics (one row per group, or one row total). mutate() transforms or creates columns while preserving the same number of rows. Both can be used with or without group_by().

answer.text: summarize() collapses many rows into a summary row; mutate() adds/modifies columns while keeping all rows

feedback.text: Correct! summarize() reduces the data to summary statistics (one row per group, or one row total). mutate() transforms or creates columns while preserving the same number of rows. Both can be used with or without group_by().

answer.text: summarize() works on character columns; mutate() works on numeric columns

feedback.text: Incorrect. summarize() reduces the data to summary statistics (one row per group, or one row total). mutate() transforms or creates columns while preserving the same number of rows. Both can be used with or without group_by().

answer.text: They are interchangeable and produce the same output

feedback.text: Incorrect. summarize() reduces the data to summary statistics (one row per group, or one row total). mutate() transforms or creates columns while preserving the same number of rows. Both can be used with or without group_by().

answer.text: summarize() requires group_by() while mutate() does not

feedback.text: Incorrect. summarize() reduces the data to summary statistics (one row per group, or one row total). mutate() transforms or creates columns while preserving the same number of rows. Both can be used with or without group_by().

name.text: M2Q04: You have a TB dataset and want the total number of cases per WHO region, sorted

questiontext.text: You have a TB dataset and want the total number of cases per WHO region, sorted from highest to lowest. Which pipeline is correct?

generalfeedback.text: The correct pipeline: (1) group_by(who_region) to set groups, (2) summarize(total = sum(cases)) to aggregate, (3) arrange(desc(total)) to sort descending. This is a fundamental dplyr workflow.

feedback.text: Correct! The correct pipeline: (1) group_by(who_region) to set groups, (2) summarize(total = sum(cases)) to aggregate, (3) arrange(desc(total)) to sort descending. This is a fundamental dplyr workflow.

Incorrect. The correct pipeline: (1) group_by(who_region) to set groups, (2) summarize(total = sum(cases)) to aggregate, (3) arrange(desc(total)) to sort descending. This is a fundamental dplyr workflow.

Incorrect. The correct pipeline: (1) group_by(who_region) to set groups, (2) summarize(total = sum(cases)) to aggregate, (3) arrange(desc(total)) to sort descending. This is a fundamental dplyr workflow.

Incorrect. The correct pipeline: (1) group_by(who_region) to set groups, (2) summarize(total = sum(cases)) to aggregate, (3) arrange(desc(total)) to sort descending. This is a fundamental dplyr workflow.

name.text: M2Q05: What does the following R code produce? df |> count(state, sort = TRUE)

questiontext.text: What does the following R code produce? df |> count(state, sort = TRUE)

generalfeedback.text: count() is shorthand for group_by() |> summarize(n = n()). With sort = TRUE, it returns the frequency table sorted in descending order. It’s extremely useful for quick data exploration.

answer.text: A frequency table of the ‘state’ column, sorted from most to least frequent

feedback.text: Correct! count() is shorthand for group_by() |> summarize(n = n()). With sort = TRUE, it returns the frequency table sorted in descending order. It’s extremely useful for quick data exploration.

answer.text: A filtered dataset containing only the state column

feedback.text: Incorrect. count() is shorthand for group_by() |> summarize(n = n()). With sort = TRUE, it returns the frequency table sorted in descending order. It’s extremely useful for quick data exploration.

answer.text: An error because count() requires two arguments

feedback.text: Incorrect. count() is shorthand for group_by() |> summarize(n = n()). With sort = TRUE, it returns the frequency table sorted in descending order. It’s extremely useful for quick data exploration.

answer.text: A random sample of states from the dataset

feedback.text: Incorrect. count() is shorthand for group_by() |> summarize(n = n()). With sort = TRUE, it returns the frequency table sorted in descending order. It’s extremely useful for quick data exploration.

name.text: M2Q06: In dplyr, left_join(x, y, by = ‘state’) will:

generalfeedback.text: left_join() preserves all rows from the left (first) table and adds matching information from the right table. Non-matching rows get NA. This is the most common join in spatial data science — keeping all geographic units even if attribute data is missing.

answer.text: Keep all rows from x and add matching columns from y, with NA for non-matches

feedback.text: Correct! left_join() preserves all rows from the left (first) table and adds matching information from the right table. Non-matching rows get NA. This is the most common join in spatial data science — keeping all geographic units even if attribute data is missing.

answer.text: Keep only rows that exist in both x and y

feedback.text: Incorrect. left_join() preserves all rows from the left (first) table and adds matching information from the right table. Non-matching rows get NA. This is the most common join in spatial data science — keeping all geographic units even if attribute data is missing.

answer.text: Keep all rows from y and add matching columns from x

feedback.text: Incorrect. left_join() preserves all rows from the left (first) table and adds matching information from the right table. Non-matching rows get NA. This is the most common join in spatial data science — keeping all geographic units even if attribute data is missing.

Incorrect. left_join() preserves all rows from the left (first) table and adds matching information from the right table. Non-matching rows get NA. This is the most common join in spatial data science — keeping all geographic units even if attribute data is missing.

name.text: M2Q07: You have wide-format data with columns ‘nfhs4’ and ‘nfhs5’ containing values for

questiontext.text: You have wide-format data with columns ‘nfhs4’ and ‘nfhs5’ containing values for two survey rounds. To create a ggplot comparing both rounds, you should first:

generalfeedback.text: For comparative visualizations (e.g., side-by-side boxplots with fill = survey), data must be in long format: one row per observation with a column identifying the survey round. pivot_longer() reshapes wide to long.

feedback.text: Correct! For comparative visualizations (e.g., side-by-side boxplots with fill = survey), data must be in long format: one row per observation with a column identifying the survey round. pivot_longer() reshapes wide to long.

Incorrect. For comparative visualizations (e.g., side-by-side boxplots with fill = survey), data must be in long format: one row per observation with a column identifying the survey round. pivot_longer() reshapes wide to long.

answer.text: Use filter() to select one survey at a time

feedback.text: Incorrect. For comparative visualizations (e.g., side-by-side boxplots with fill = survey), data must be in long format: one row per observation with a column identifying the survey round. pivot_longer() reshapes wide to long.

answer.text: Use mutate() to combine both columns into one

feedback.text: Incorrect. For comparative visualizations (e.g., side-by-side boxplots with fill = survey), data must be in long format: one row per observation with a column identifying the survey round. pivot_longer() reshapes wide to long.

name.text: M2Q08: Which case_when() expression correctly recodes state names?

questiontext.text: Which case_when() expression correctly recodes state names?

generalfeedback.text: case_when() requires a TRUE ~ state fallback clause to preserve values that don’t match any condition. Without it, unmatched values become NA. The second option would lose all states except Orissa and Pondicherry.

feedback.text: Correct! case_when() requires a TRUE ~ state fallback clause to preserve values that don’t match any condition. Without it, unmatched values become NA. The second option would lose all states except Orissa and Pondicherry.

Incorrect. case_when() requires a TRUE ~ state fallback clause to preserve values that don’t match any condition. Without it, unmatched values become NA. The second option would lose all states except Orissa and Pondicherry.

Incorrect. case_when() requires a TRUE ~ state fallback clause to preserve values that don’t match any condition. Without it, unmatched values become NA. The second option would lose all states except Orissa and Pondicherry.

Incorrect. case_when() requires a TRUE ~ state fallback clause to preserve values that don’t match any condition. Without it, unmatched values become NA. The second option would lose all states except Orissa and Pondicherry.

name.text: M2Q09: The function janitor::clean_names() is used to:

generalfeedback.text: clean_names() converts messy column names (e.g., ‘Name And Address Of Medical College’) to clean snake_case format (e.g., ‘name_and_address_of_medical_college’). Essential first step when importing external data.

answer.text: Standardize column names to snake_case (lowercase with underscores)

feedback.text: Correct! clean_names() converts messy column names (e.g., ‘Name And Address Of Medical College’) to clean snake_case format (e.g., ‘name_and_address_of_medical_college’). Essential first step when importing external data.

Incorrect. clean_names() converts messy column names (e.g., ‘Name And Address Of Medical College’) to clean snake_case format (e.g., ‘name_and_address_of_medical_college’). Essential first step when importing external data.

Incorrect. clean_names() converts messy column names (e.g., ‘Name And Address Of Medical College’) to clean snake_case format (e.g., ‘name_and_address_of_medical_college’). Essential first step when importing external data.

Incorrect. clean_names() converts messy column names (e.g., ‘Name And Address Of Medical College’) to clean snake_case format (e.g., ‘name_and_address_of_medical_college’). Essential first step when importing external data.

name.text: M2Q10: According to John Tukey (1977), Exploratory Data Analysis is best described as:

questiontext.text: According to John Tukey (1977), Exploratory Data Analysis is best described as:

generalfeedback.text: Tukey described EDA as ‘detective work’ — using questions as tools to guide investigation. It is an iterative, creative process that generates hypotheses rather than testing pre-specified ones.

answer.text: A philosophy of examining data without preconceived ideas to discover what the data can reveal

feedback.text: Correct! Tukey described EDA as ‘detective work’ — using questions as tools to guide investigation. It is an iterative, creative process that generates hypotheses rather than testing pre-specified ones.

answer.text: A strict statistical protocol for hypothesis testing

feedback.text: Incorrect. Tukey described EDA as ‘detective work’ — using questions as tools to guide investigation. It is an iterative, creative process that generates hypotheses rather than testing pre-specified ones.

answer.text: A method for proving causal relationships in observational data

feedback.text: Incorrect. Tukey described EDA as ‘detective work’ — using questions as tools to guide investigation. It is an iterative, creative process that generates hypotheses rather than testing pre-specified ones.

answer.text: A technique specific to spatial data that cannot be applied to tabular data

feedback.text: Incorrect. Tukey described EDA as ‘detective work’ — using questions as tools to guide investigation. It is an iterative, creative process that generates hypotheses rather than testing pre-specified ones.

name.text: M2Q11: To view the structure of a tibble (column names, types, and first few values), w

questiontext.text: To view the structure of a tibble (column names, types, and first few values), which function is most appropriate?

generalfeedback.text: glimpse() from dplyr/tibble provides a transposed view showing column names, data types, and the first few values of each column. It’s more informative than str() for tibbles and is the recommended first look at imported data.

feedback.text: Correct! glimpse() from dplyr/tibble provides a transposed view showing column names, data types, and the first few values of each column. It’s more informative than str() for tibbles and is the recommended first look at imported data.

Incorrect. glimpse() from dplyr/tibble provides a transposed view showing column names, data types, and the first few values of each column. It’s more informative than str() for tibbles and is the recommended first look at imported data.

Incorrect. glimpse() from dplyr/tibble provides a transposed view showing column names, data types, and the first few values of each column. It’s more informative than str() for tibbles and is the recommended first look at imported data.

Incorrect. glimpse() from dplyr/tibble provides a transposed view showing column names, data types, and the first few values of each column. It’s more informative than str() for tibbles and is the recommended first look at imported data.

name.text: M2Q12: The 6 W’s of Spatial EDA include: What, Where, When, Who, Why, and How. The ‘Whe

questiontext.text: The 6 W’s of Spatial EDA include: What, Where, When, Who, Why, and How. The ‘Where’ dimension specifically asks:

generalfeedback.text: In Spatial EDA, ‘Where’ examines the geographic distribution of the variable: Are there clusters? Is there spatial heterogeneity? Do urban/rural patterns differ? This is what distinguishes Spatial EDA from standard EDA.

answer.text: What is the geographic distribution and spatial pattern of the health variable?

feedback.text: Correct! In Spatial EDA, ‘Where’ examines the geographic distribution of the variable: Are there clusters? Is there spatial heterogeneity? Do urban/rural patterns differ? This is what distinguishes Spatial EDA from standard EDA.

Incorrect. In Spatial EDA, ‘Where’ examines the geographic distribution of the variable: Are there clusters? Is there spatial heterogeneity? Do urban/rural patterns differ? This is what distinguishes Spatial EDA from standard EDA.

answer.text: Where in the code should I place the analysis function?

feedback.text: Incorrect. In Spatial EDA, ‘Where’ examines the geographic distribution of the variable: Are there clusters? Is there spatial heterogeneity? Do urban/rural patterns differ? This is what distinguishes Spatial EDA from standard EDA.

Incorrect. In Spatial EDA, ‘Where’ examines the geographic distribution of the variable: Are there clusters? Is there spatial heterogeneity? Do urban/rural patterns differ? This is what distinguishes Spatial EDA from standard EDA.

name.text: M2Q13: The %in% operator in R checks whether each element of the left vector exists any

questiontext.text: The %in% operator in R checks whether each element of the left vector exists anywhere in the right vector.

generalfeedback.text: x %in% y returns a logical vector of the same length as x, with TRUE for elements that match any value in y. It’s essential for checking name matches between datasets (e.g., verifying state names exist in both spatial and attribute data).

feedback.text: Correct! x %in% y returns a logical vector of the same length as x, with TRUE for elements that match any value in y. It’s essential for checking name matches between datasets (e.g., verifying state names exist in both spatial and attribute data).

Incorrect. x %in% y returns a logical vector of the same length as x, with TRUE for elements that match any value in y. It’s essential for checking name matches between datasets (e.g., verifying state names exist in both spatial and attribute data).

name.text: M2Q14: You want to create a boxplot comparing TB incidence across WHO regions with diff

questiontext.text: You want to create a boxplot comparing TB incidence across WHO regions with different colors per region. Which ggplot2 code is correct?

generalfeedback.text: In ggplot2, aesthetic mappings (aes()) connect data variables to visual properties. x maps to the category axis, y to the value axis, and fill maps color by group. Aesthetics that vary by data must go inside aes().

answer.text: ggplot(aes(x = who_region, y = incidence_100k, fill = who_region)) + geom_boxplot()

feedback.text: Correct! In ggplot2, aesthetic mappings (aes()) connect data variables to visual properties. x maps to the category axis, y to the value axis, and fill maps color by group. Aesthetics that vary by data must go inside aes().

Incorrect. In ggplot2, aesthetic mappings (aes()) connect data variables to visual properties. x maps to the category axis, y to the value axis, and fill maps color by group. Aesthetics that vary by data must go inside aes().

Incorrect. In ggplot2, aesthetic mappings (aes()) connect data variables to visual properties. x maps to the category axis, y to the value axis, and fill maps color by group. Aesthetics that vary by data must go inside aes().

Incorrect. In ggplot2, aesthetic mappings (aes()) connect data variables to visual properties. x maps to the category axis, y to the value axis, and fill maps color by group. Aesthetics that vary by data must go inside aes().

name.text: M2Q15: According to CDC cartographic guidelines, the principle of ‘visual contrast’ in

questiontext.text: According to CDC cartographic guidelines, the principle of ‘visual contrast’ in map design means:

generalfeedback.text: Visual contrast ensures readers can easily distinguish different features, classes, and labels. This involves choosing appropriate color schemes, line weights, and text sizes so that the map is immediately readable.

answer.text: Map features should be clearly distinguishable from each other and from the background

feedback.text: Correct! Visual contrast ensures readers can easily distinguish different features, classes, and labels. This involves choosing appropriate color schemes, line weights, and text sizes so that the map is immediately readable.

answer.text: Maps should always use black and white only

feedback.text: Incorrect. Visual contrast ensures readers can easily distinguish different features, classes, and labels. This involves choosing appropriate color schemes, line weights, and text sizes so that the map is immediately readable.

answer.text: High-value areas must always be shown in red

feedback.text: Incorrect. Visual contrast ensures readers can easily distinguish different features, classes, and labels. This involves choosing appropriate color schemes, line weights, and text sizes so that the map is immediately readable.

answer.text: The legend should use the same color as the map background

feedback.text: Incorrect. Visual contrast ensures readers can easily distinguish different features, classes, and labels. This involves choosing appropriate color schemes, line weights, and text sizes so that the map is immediately readable.
